required.title: [Book] Home Comforts: The Art and Science of Keeping House
required.author: Cheryl Mendelson

suggested.title: [Book] Clean My Space: The Secret to Cleaning Better, Faster, and Loving Your Home Every Day
suggested.author: Melissa Maker

suggested.title: [Book] The Life-Changing Magic of Tidying Up
suggested.author: Marie Kondo

supplementary.title: [Book] Unf
supplementary.author: ck Your Habitat* by Rachel Hoffman

competence_markers.description: The student can independently maintain a household cleaning schedule — daily, weekly, and monthly tasks completed on time
The student can explain basic cleaning chemistry — how soap works, the difference between cleaning and disinfecting, what never to mix
The student manages their own laundry completely — sorting, washing, stain treatment, drying, folding, put-away
The student can manage one full household laundry day per week
The student can deep-clean any room (bathroom, kitchen, bedroom) to a standard that passes inspection
The student has designed and implemented at least one organizational system that improves a household space
The student can identify sanitation risks in a kitchen and explain proper food-contact surface cleaning
The student understands the connection between cleanliness and health and can articulate it

cross_connections.description: Cleaning chemistry is applied chemistry. Soap, disinfectants, acids, bases — the student is using chemical principles in daily life.
Maintaining a cleaning schedule requires doing the work on schedule whether you feel like it or not. This is discipline in its most domestic form.
A clean home reveals maintenance needs. The student who deep-cleans the bathroom notices the leaking faucet. Cleaning and maintenance overlap constantly.
Kitchen sanitation after cooking is both a food safety skill and a cleaning skill.
Cleaning the parts nobody sees — behind the toilet, under the couch, inside the oven — is integrity applied. Do you maintain the standard when no one is checking?

content.why_this_matters:
You know how to keep your own space clean. Now you are going to learn to clean a house — not as a chore you are assigned, but as a system you understand and can execute. When you understand cleaning as a system, you stop seeing it as an endless, reactive battle against dirt and start seeing it as a manageable routine that keeps a home functioning.

You will learn why cleaning works — the chemistry behind soap, disinfectants, and the difference between "looks clean" and "is clean." You will learn to manage your own laundry from start to finish. You will learn to deep-clean any room in a house to a standard that would pass a rigorous inspection. And you will build organizational systems that work — not because they look nice, but because they make life run smoothly.

A person who can maintain a clean, organized home is a person whose environment supports their work, their health, and their relationships. A person who cannot is fighting their own surroundings every day and usually does not even realize it.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Systematic cleaning routines** — daily, weekly, monthly, and seasonal cleaning schedules with the logic behind each
2. **Cleaning chemistry** — why soap works, what disinfectants do, the difference between clean and sanitized
3. **Laundry competence** — sorting, washing, drying, folding, ironing, stain treatment, fabric care
4. **Deep cleaning skills** — bathrooms, kitchens, floors, windows, appliances to a high standard
5. **Organization systems** — designing storage and organization for retrieval efficiency, not aesthetics
6. **Sanitation and health awareness** — the connection between cleanliness and disease prevention
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Cleaning as a System

Cleaning fails when it is reactive — when you clean only because something is visibly dirty or because someone is coming over. Reactive cleaning is exhausting because by the time dirt is visible, the problem is already significant, and you are always behind.

Systematic cleaning is proactive. It happens on a schedule, regardless of whether the space looks dirty. The logic: regular light maintenance prevents the need for heavy emergency cleaning.

**The schedule framework:**

**Daily (10-15 minutes total):**
- Make beds
- Wash dishes / run dishwasher
- Wipe kitchen counters and stovetop
- Quick sweep or vacuum of high-traffic areas
- 10-minute general tidying

**Weekly (2-3 hours total, can be distributed across the week):**
- Vacuum all floors
- Mop hard floors
- Clean bathrooms (toilet, sink, shower/tub, mirror, floor)
- Dust surfaces
- Change bed linens
- Laundry (may be more frequent depending on household size)
- Take out all trash and recycling
- Wipe kitchen appliance exteriors

**Monthly:**
- Clean inside refrigerator
- Clean oven
- Wash windows (interior)
- Dust ceiling fans, light fixtures, vents
- Clean behind and under furniture
- Organize one closet or storage area

**Seasonal (quarterly):**
- Deep clean carpets
- Wash curtains/drapes
- Clean windows (exterior)
- Clean gutters (connects to 2.3.3)
- Rotate seasonal items (clothes, decorations)
- Full decluttering pass

### Part 2: Cleaning Chemistry

Understanding why cleaning products work means you can improvise with basic ingredients, avoid dangerous combinations, and spend less money on branded products that do the same thing as simpler alternatives.

**How soap works:** Soap molecules have two ends — one that attracts water (hydrophilic) and one that attracts oil and grease (hydrophobic). When you wash with soap, the oil-attracting ends attach to grease and dirt, and the water-attracting ends pull them into the rinse water. Soap does not kill bacteria — it removes them from surfaces along with the dirt they cling to.

**Disinfectants versus cleaners:** Cleaning removes dirt and organic matter. Disinfecting kills microorganisms. These are different processes. A surface that looks clean is not necessarily disinfected. A surface that has been sprayed with disinfectant but not cleaned first may not be effectively disinfected — the dirt shields bacteria from the disinfectant. The correct order: clean first, then disinfect.

**Common disinfectants:**
- **Bleach (sodium hypochlorite):** Effective, cheap, widely available. Kills virtually all bacteria and viruses. Must be diluted (typically 1 tablespoon per gallon of water for general disinfection). Contact time matters — the surface must stay wet with bleach solution for at least one minute to be effective.
- **Isopropyl alcohol (70%):** Effective against many bacteria and viruses. Evaporates quickly. Good for electronics and small surface areas.
- **Hydrogen peroxide (3%):** Effective disinfectant. Breaks down into water and oxygen. Good alternative for people who prefer to avoid bleach.
- **Vinegar:** Popular as a "natural cleaner." Effective at cutting grease and dissolving mineral deposits. Weak as a disinfectant. Do not rely on vinegar for sanitation — it does not kill most harmful pathogens.

**NEVER mix bleach with ammonia or acids (including vinegar).** Bleach + ammonia produces toxic chloramine gas. Bleach + vinegar or other acids produces chlorine gas. Both can cause serious injury or death. This is not a theoretical warning — it happens in homes every year.

### Part 3: Laundry

Laundry is a complete cycle: sort, wash, dry, fold, put away. Every step matters. Skipping any step (especially the last two) results in the common disaster of clean clothes living permanently in baskets or dryers.

**Sorting:** Separate by color (whites, darks, colors) and by fabric weight (heavy items like jeans and towels; lighter items like shirts and underwear). Wash heavily soiled items separately. Check all pockets before washing.

**Washing:** Use the right water temperature — hot for whites and heavily soiled items, cold for darks and delicates. Cold water is sufficient for most loads and saves energy. Use the right amount of detergent — more is not better. Excess detergent leaves residue on clothes and inside the machine.

**Stain treatment:** Treat stains before washing. Blood: cold water (hot sets blood stains permanently). Grease: dish soap or laundry pre-treat. Ink: rubbing alcohol. Grass: enzyme-based stain remover. Red wine: salt, then cold water. The universal rule: treat as soon as possible; do not put stained items in the dryer (heat sets most stains permanently).

**Drying:** Read care labels. Some items cannot go in the dryer (wool, silk, some synthetics). Hang-drying is gentler on clothes and free. Clean the lint trap before every dryer load — a clogged lint trap is a fire hazard.

**Folding and putting away:** This is where most people fail. Laundry is not done when it comes out of the dryer. It is done when it is folded and in the drawer or closet. Do this immediately — letting clean laundry sit in a basket or on a bed is a habit that compounds quickly.

**Ironing:** Not every garment needs ironing. But you should know how: set the iron to the correct temperature for the fabric, use steam for cotton and linen, iron in long smooth strokes, start with collars and cuffs on shirts. A wrinkled shirt communicates carelessness. A pressed shirt communicates standards.

### Part 4: Deep Cleaning

Deep cleaning means cleaning beyond the surface — the places regular cleaning does not reach. Every room in a house needs periodic deep cleaning.

**Bathroom deep clean:**
1. Remove all items from surfaces and shower
2. Apply toilet bowl cleaner; let it sit
3. Spray all surfaces with appropriate cleaner (tile cleaner for shower, all-purpose for counters)
4. Scrub shower/tub — tile, grout, fixtures, door or curtain
5. Scrub sink and counter
6. Scrub toilet — inside bowl, outside, base, behind
7. Clean mirror
8. Mop floor, including behind toilet and around edges
9. Replace items, fresh towels

**Kitchen deep clean:**
1. Clean inside oven (self-clean cycle or oven cleaner with ventilation)
2. Clean inside microwave (steam with water and vinegar, then wipe)
3. Pull out refrigerator, clean behind and underneath
4. Clean inside refrigerator — remove all items, wipe shelves, check for expired foods
5. Clean range hood and filter
6. Scrub sink thoroughly, including drain
7. Wipe all cabinet fronts
8. Clean and organize pantry
9. Mop floor, including under appliances if accessible

### Part 5: Organization Systems

Organization is not about making spaces look pretty. It is about making spaces work. The test of an organizational system is retrieval speed: can you find what you need within 30 seconds? If not, the system has failed.

**Principles:**
- **Frequency drives placement.** Items you use daily should be the most accessible. Items you use once a year go in the back or on the highest shelf.
- **Categories, not locations.** All cleaning supplies in one place. All tools in one place. All first aid supplies in one place. Do not scatter related items across multiple locations.
- **Containers and labels.** Clear containers so you can see contents. Labels so anyone in the household can find and return items. A system that only one person understands is not a system — it is a memory.
- **One in, one out.** When a new item enters the household, a similar item leaves. This prevents gradual accumulation that overwhelms any organizational system.

### Part 6: Sanitation and Health

Clean homes have fewer sick days. This is not metaphor — it is epidemiology.

The connection between cleanliness and health is direct: pathogens (bacteria, viruses, parasites) thrive on dirty surfaces, in standing water, in food residue, and in environments where organic matter accumulates. Regular cleaning physically removes pathogens. Disinfection kills those that remain.

**High-touch surfaces** — door handles, light switches, faucet handles, toilet flush handles, remote controls, phones — carry the highest pathogen load because everyone touches them and few people clean them regularly. Disinfecting these surfaces weekly (or daily during illness) significantly reduces disease transmission in a household.

**Kitchen sanitation** is the most critical domestic sanitation practice. Raw meat, poultry, and eggs carry bacteria (salmonella, E. coli, campylobacter) that cause serious illness. Cutting boards, knives, and counters that contact raw meat must be cleaned with hot soapy water and then disinfected. Cross-contamination — raw meat juice contacting ready-to-eat food — is the most common home food safety failure.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Schedule Adherence
**Developing well:** The student follows their cleaning schedule independently. Tasks are completed on time. The house is consistently maintained.

**Developing slowly:** The student completes tasks only when reminded or lets the schedule slip after the first week.

### 2. Deep Cleaning Quality
**Developing well:** Inspection reveals no missed areas. Surfaces are genuinely clean, not just wiped. Grout is scrubbed. Behind fixtures is addressed. The student self-evaluates before asking for inspection.

**Developing slowly:** Surface-level cleaning only. Missed spots are common. The student does not check their own work.

### 3. Laundry Independence
**Developing well:** Laundry cycle completes same-day — sorted, washed, dried, folded, put away. No stains missed. No shrinkage from wrong temperatures. Clean clothes are in drawers, not baskets.

**Developing slowly:** Laundry sits in the dryer or in baskets. Stains are not pre-treated. Colors are mixed with whites.

### 4. Chemistry Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can explain how soap works, why disinfection is different from cleaning, and what should never be mixed. They can improvise cleaning solutions from basic ingredients.

**Developing slowly:** The student uses products by brand name without understanding what they do or why.
